primary.notes: Calibration fixture 'calibration-thought_leader' with single THOUGHT_LEADER draft (1647 chars, 4 hashtags). Structural gate would pass: char_count inside [1500,2500], hashtags inside [3,5], length_bracket consistent with declared bracket. Grounding gate likely passes: lived-work claims name 'gofreddy' (the author's own project) with PR-attribution in META block; no unnamed-entity 'we' specifics that would trip HARD FLOOR. LI-6 scored 5 (data-limited) because cohort has n=1 — flagged so calibration aggregator can either exclude this dimension or treat consistently across runs. Aggregate is unweighted arithmetic mean across 6 criteria; if calibration uses geometric mean or weighted mean, expect a slightly different headline number from the same per-criterion breakdown.

per_criterion.rationale: Thoughtful-authority register throughout. Story-led opener ('A few weeks ago a colleague asked'); first-person lived-work specifics (gofreddy, 149 lenses, 47 hours); plain-English even when introducing technical terms ('verifier that re-runs every finding'). No contrarian declaratives, no sub-200-char X-style sharps, no 'Most marketers don't realize...' hot-takes. Tone is 'here's what I keep returning to' not 'you're doing this wrong.' Slight deduction: 'Three answers I keep returning to' is mildly formulaic but stays inside the LinkedIn voice envelope rather than tipping into bait.

SOURCE/INTERPRETIVE split is clean. Lived-work claims name the project entity ('gofreddy', 'gofreddy's own pipeline') with attribution to PR #45 squash 0543d7b in the META block. Specific numbers (149 lenses, 25 vertical bundles, 33 findings, 20 verified, 47 hours, 36 findings, 11 areas, 9 sections) match the project shape. Two reasons not to score 8+: (1) the 'somewhere around fifty cents' compute cost is the only unhedged-yet-precise specific that isn't traceable in the artifact, and (2) without programs/references/voice.md visible in the fixture I cannot verify the HARD-FLOOR named-entity check — gofreddy is plainly the author's own project so HARD FLOOR is unlikely to fire, but I score conservatively rather than assume.

Opening is ~115 chars and beats the 210 desktop show-more cutoff. Story-led ('A few weeks ago a colleague asked: how does an AI-native agency actually price work?') with a real question that earns line two. Not contrarian, not bait, not engagement-prompting. Below 8 because the colleague is generic ('a colleague') rather than a specific named or numerically-anchored hook — compare to the rubric's gold-standard example which leads with a concrete number ('47 hours of agent debugging led to one config change'). The number-led version of this same insight would score higher; the question-led version lands but is one notch less arresting.

No banned-phrase regex hits I can detect: no 'Game-changer', no 'Here's what I learned' close, no 'Thoughts? 👇', no emoji bait, no fake 'Hot take:' framing, no whitespace-inflation paragraph breaks. However two thought-leadership cadence patterns bleed through: (1) 'Three answers I keep returning to' is a soft variant of the 'Three takeaways:' pattern called out in the rubric, and (2) the 'If you take one thing away:' close is a formulaic LinkedIn-AI transition marker in the same family as 'here's the thing.' Voice underneath is JR-shaped but the LinkedIn-AI rhythm is audible at the seams. Per rubric: 'one or two LinkedIn-AI patterns slip through' = 3, scaled up to 6 because the patterns are mild, not stacked, and the closing line itself is substantive.

THOUGHT_LEADER bracket = 1500-2500 chars; draft is 1647 ✓. Structural beats present: story-opening → frame ('Three answers') → 3 substantive numbered points → implication close ('AI-native pricing does not look like agency-hours pricing'). Hashtag count = 4 (#B2BMarketing, #AIagents, #MarketingOps, #AgencyPricing) — inside the ideal [3,5] range, no zero-tag penalty, no spam guardrail risk. Hashtags map to topical pillars rather than generic '#marketing'. Below 9 because the three points lean lecture-cadence rather than narrative inside each point — a CASE_STUDY-flavored second point would push to 9; as written it's solid bracket-aware structure but not 'mastery'-level.

Single-draft cohort. The fixture exposes exactly one artifact (drafts/thought_leader.md); cohort-fit cannot be evaluated against itself. Per instructions ('score only what is in front of you'), I do not assume sibling drafts. With n=1 the geometric-mean construction in the rubric is undefined for cross-archetype variance — I report the neutral midpoint. This is a calibration-fixture artifact, not a session-cohort artifact, so I treat LI-6 as data-limited and flag it in notes rather than penalize the draft for cohort properties it cannot demonstrate.

secondary.notes: Strong LinkedIn structure and opening, but grounding is weak because required source/voice artifacts are not present, and cohort diversity cannot be established from a single draft.

per_criterion.rationale: The draft is mostly LinkedIn-appropriate: first-person adjacent, story-led from a colleague question, and written in a professional B2B register. Evidence includes 'A few weeks ago a colleague asked' and the practical pricing frame for CMOs. It does lean somewhat systems-heavy with terms like 'deterministic check,' 'LLM call,' 'multi-cycle harness,' and 'LHR-style report' without much plain-English follow-up, which caps the voice below excellent.

The draft contains many specific factual and lived-work claims: '149 always-on lenses,' '25 vertical bundles,' 'fifty cents,' '33 actionable findings,' '20 of them in 47 hours,' and '36-finding deliverable across 11 marketing areas.' The only grounding visible is a meta attribution to 'gofreddy harness PR #45 squash 0543d7b,' but no source_text or voice.md artifact is provided. Because the required grounding artifact is absent from what is in front of me, these claims are not independently traceable here.

The opening earns the next line without using a contrarian hot-take: 'A few weeks ago a colleague asked: how does an AI-native agency actually price work?' It is specific enough to establish a lived-work context and creates practical before-the-fold tension. It is not as strong as a concrete-result opener, but it fits LinkedIn well.

There are no obvious hard LinkedIn-AI tells like 'Thoughts?', emoji engagement bait, 'Hot take:', or whitespace padding. However, 'Three answers I keep returning to' creates a familiar thought-leadership/list cadence, and 'If you take one thing away' is somewhat formulaic. The draft mostly avoids AI rhythm, but not completely.

The thought_leader structure fits the declared bracket: story/question opening, clear frame, three substantive numbered-style points, and an implication close. It has 4 targeted hashtags in meta: B2BMarketing, AIagents, MarketingOps, AgencyPricing. The structure earns its 1647 characters, though the three-point format is conventional rather than especially distinctive.

Only one draft artifact is provided, so cohort-level archetype variation cannot be demonstrated. The visible draft uses a lesson-led/list frame with a story/question opener, and the only visible voice_pillar is harness-engineering. Scoring only what is in front of me, the cohort does not show range across archetypes or pillars.
